initial_program:
% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Transformation rule:
%   * The highest colour (4) forms the wall.
%   * Every cell gets a colour equal to (4  Manhattan distance)
%     to the nearest wall cell (minimum distance  highest colour).
%   * Cells farther than distance 4 become 0.
%   * The output grid is exactly the set of cells for which the
%     formula yields a colour in 0..4; all other cells are 0.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% All colour values that may appear (0..9 are injected automatically)
color(0). color(1). color(2). color(3). color(4). color(5).
color(6). color(7). color(8). color(9).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  1.  Identify the wall cells  the cells that originally contain
%      the maximum colour (4).  In the given puzzles these are the
%      four corner cells, but the rule works for any set of wall
%      cells.
% --------------------------------------------------------------
wall(R,C) :-
    input(R,C,4).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  2.  Manhattan distance from any cell to a wall cell.
%      ManhattanDist(R,C,D) is the distance to the *nearest* wall.
%      We first compute the distance to each wall cell, then we
%      choose the smallest distance (i.e. the largest colour).
% --------------------------------------------------------------
dist(R,C,0) :-
    wall(Wr,Cw),
    R = Wr, C = Cw.               % distance 0 for a wall cell

dist(R,C,D) :-
    wall(Wr,Cw),
    R != Wr,
    C != Cw,
    D1 = abs(R - Wr),
    D2 = abs(C - Cw),
    D3 = D1 + D2,
    D = D3.

% Helper for absolute difference.
abs(X,Y,Z) :-
    X >= Y, Z = X - Y.
abs(X,Y,Z) :-
    X <  Y, Z = Y - X.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  3.  Derive the output colour.
%      colour = max(0, 4 - distance).
% --------------------------------------------------------------
output(R,C,V) :-
    dist(R,C,D),
    V = 4 - D,
    V >= 0,
    color(V).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  4.  Exactly one colour per cell.
% --------------------------------------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- true.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Transformation rule:
%   * The highest colour (4) forms the wall.
%   * Every cell gets a colour equal to (4  Manhattan distance)
%     to the nearest wall cell (minimum distance  highest colour).
%   * Cells farther than distance 4 become 0.
%   * The output grid is exactly the set of cells for which the
%     formula yields a colour in 0..4; all other cells are 0.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% All colour values that may appear (0..9 are injected automatically)
color(0). color(1). color(2). color(3). color(4). color(5).
color(6). color(7). color(8). color(9).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  1.  Identify the wall cells  the cells that originally contain
%      the maximum colour (4).  In the given puzzles these are the
%      four corner cells, but the rule works for any set of wall
%      cells.
% --------------------------------------------------------------
wall(R,C) :-
    input(R,C,4).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  2.  Manhattan distance from any cell to a wall cell.
%      ManhattanDist(R,C,D) is the distance to the *nearest* wall.
%      We first compute the distance to each wall cell, then we
%      choose the smallest distance (i.e. the largest colour).
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 2.  Manhattan distance from any cell to a wall cell and compute colour.
output(R,C,V) :- wall(Wr,Cw), V = 4 - (abs(R - Wr) + abs(C - Cw)), V >= 0, color(V).
% Helper for absolute difference.
abs(X,Y,Z) :- X >= Y, Z = X - Y.
ab(X,Y,Z) :- X < Y, Z = Y - X.
positive_abs(X,Y,Z) :- true.
cell(R,C) :- true.
% Exactly one colour per cell.
1 { output(R,C,Col), cell(R,C) : color(Col) } 1 :- true.
